steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is an Inferences question. The passage sets up a contrast: CLPs are shielded from external conditions during transmission, while directly transmitted parasites are exposed to them. The unexpected finding is that CLP abundance decreased with rising temperatures while directly transmitted parasite abundance stayed stable. The blank must explain how this surprising result follows from the evidence.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — any advantages that the transmission strategy used by three host CLPs may have conferred did not completely offset the negative effects of other temperature-driven factors on CLP abundance.: The shielding advantage is real, but CLP abundance still dropped—meaning the advantage existed yet was insufficient to counteract other temperature-driven harms. This directly resolves the paradox without adding unsupported claims. ✓
  • Choice B — CLPs primarily transmitted by ingestion were less dependent on host species adversely affected by warming temperatures than were CLPs that use other transmission strategies.: The passage does not distinguish among CLP transmission strategies or compare ingestion-based CLPs to other CLPs. This distinction is invented and unsupported by the data. ✗
  • Choice C — as the number of host species involved in a parasite's transmission increases, the parasite is better protected against rising temperatures.: The data show the opposite: three-host CLPs (more host species) declined, while single-host directly transmitted parasites were stable. This contradicts the evidence. ✗
  • Choice D — directly transmitted parasites identified in the study were more likely to use transmission strategies that shield them from warming temperatures than were three-host CLPs.: The passage explicitly states that directly transmitted parasites are exposed to external conditions during transmission—not shielded. This directly contradicts the passage. ✗
That matches Choice A — any advantages that the transmission strategy used by three host CLPs may have conferred did not completely offset the negative effects of other temperature-driven factors on CLP abundance..
